exemplar.title: To what extent have Stella McCartneyâs (SMC) business policies and practices from 2022 onwards been ethical?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent have Stella McCartneyâs (SMC) business policies and practices from 2022 onwards been ethical?

subject.title: Business Management
subject.description:
**IB Business Management** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **business fundamentals, human resource management, finance and accounts, marketing, and operations management**, with a strong focus on **case study analysis, real-world business applications, and strategic decision-making**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2024**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| **Assessment** | **Format** | **SL** | **HL** | **Weighting** |
|----------------|------------|--------|--------|---------------|
| **Paper 1** | Case study-based exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 25% |
| **Paper 2** | Core themes exam | 1h 30min | 1h 30min | SL: 35%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | Social enterprise case study | N/A | 1h 15min | HL: 25% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Written commentary (SL) / Research project (HL) | Various | Various | SL: 30%, HL: 20% |

strengths: Explicitly stating âKey Concept: Ethicsâ at the outset, guiding the analytical lens throughout the report.
Consistent return to ethics when discussing suppliers, supply-chain workers, CSR and circular business models, showing strong conceptual focus.
weaknesses: Discussion often shifts into description of actions rather than analysing how these actions exemplify or conflict with ethical norms.
Definition of ethics remains surface-level and is not underpinned by established ethical frameworks (e.g., utilitarianism, deontology).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates a strong understanding of ethics and maintains clear linkage to Stella McCartney throughout the report. However, the analysis of how specific actions reflect or challenge ethical theories is only partial. This performance aligns with band 4, meriting 4/5.

strengths: Exactly five supporting documents are cited, meeting the IB requirement.
Documents are relevant to the research question and drawn from credible sources such as SMCâs Impact Report, industry magazines and NGO websites.
The reference list is comprehensive and supports the credibility of both theoretical concepts and company data.
weaknesses: Depth is limited, largely repeating corporate claims without independent critique or alternative perspectives.
Lacks diversity of sourcesâno academic or third-party NGO reports to deepen the analysis.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student provides a complete set of five documents that are generally relevant and sufficiently in-depth. However, the insights remain largely descriptive and sourced from the company itself, placing this work solidly in band 3 for Supporting Documents (3/4).

strengths: Clear definitions of CSR and a structured categorisation of CSR impacts provide a solid foundation.
Effective linkage of the circular business model to Stella McCartneyâs initiatives demonstrates an understanding of theory-to-practice application.
weaknesses: Selection of tools and theories is limited; key frameworks such as Triple Bottom Line or stakeholder matrix are missing.
Application remains largely descriptive and lacks critical depth, fitting a superficial rather than an analytical approach.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student uses basic tools (stakeholder concept, CSR, CBM) but omits more advanced frameworks and applies them descriptively. This matches band 2 for Selection and Application of Tools and Theories (2/4).
feedback.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

strengths: Good use of quantitative data (Scope 1 & 2 emissions table) to support ethical evaluation.
Effective case study analysis of the Raffia bag design and insightful discussion of The RealReal collaboration.
Critical reflection on the decline in responsible material percentages shows integration of conflicting evidence.
weaknesses: Analysis sometimes assertive without deeper comparison, quantification or exploration of causes.
Discussion of workersâ conditions is mostly descriptive, lacking additional survey or qualitative data to substantiate claims.
Visuals (e.g., image of Raffia bag) are under-analysed; the life-cycle implications of collaborations are not fully evaluated.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student provides adequate data selection and some coherent analysis of the research question but falls short of sustained, deep integration. This corresponds to band 3 for Analysis and Evaluation (3/5).

strengths: Conclusions fully consistent with the evidence presented (emissions data, CSR initiatives, material percentages).
Explicitly answers the research question with a balanced judgement that SMC is âsubstantially ethicalâ¦yet imperfect.â
weaknesses: Lacks specific, actionable recommendations for future ethical improvements, limiting the forward-looking value of the conclusion.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion synthesises key findings, aligns them with the research question and is fully supported by the evidence. This fulfills the top band for Conclusions (3/3).

strengths: Logical sequence of sections with a clear table of contents, aiding reader navigation.
Appropriate structure throughout: introduction, analysis sections, conclusion follows IB expectations.
weaknesses: Research question scope could be more explicitly framed with the stated timeframe (e.g., âfrom 2022 onwardsâ).
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The report exhibits a clear and consistent organizational structure from start to finish, matching band 2 for Structure (2/2).

strengths: All required elements are present: title page, word count, table of contents, section headings, bibliography and in-text references.
Consistent inclusion of headings and bibliographic details supports readability and meets IB formatting requirements.
weaknesses: Inconsistent citation formatting and broken URL line breaks detract from overall professionalism.
Missing page numbers in the table of contents and inconsistent in-text citation style reduce navigability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All prescribed presentation elements are included, placing this work in band 2 for Presentation (2/2).

strandSelections.reasoning: The report follows a logical sequenceâintroduction, stakeholder analysis, CSR, CBM, conclusionâsupported by a contents table, indicating an appropriate structure throughout.

All supporting documents come from SMCâs own impact report, industry magazines or NGO websites and clearly relate to the research question; depth is reasonable but largely repeats corporate claims, offering limited independent insight, so they are ârelevant and sufficiently in-depthâ rather than providing a wide range of perspectives.
Title page, word count, table of contents, section headings, bibliography and in-text references are present, so all required elements are included.
Exactly five documents (SD1âSD5) are explicitly cited, meeting the 3-5 requirement; therefore the mid-band is appropriate.

Tools are applied to the research question but often descriptively: the stakeholder section lists interests and then comments on SMCâs actions; CSR and CBM are narrated rather than critically weighed.  Relevance is clear yet analysis remains superficial, matching the 2-mark descriptor.

Data include the Scope 1 & 2 emissions table (quantitative), references to percentage of âresponsibleâ materials, and qualitative evidence from supplier surveys.  This constitutes an âadequateâ selection and use, though not wide-ranging.

The student analyses impacts on stakeholders and evaluates contradictions (e.g. falling responsible-material percentage).  The reasoning is generally logical but sometimes assertive without deeper comparison or quantification, so quality is âsome analysisâ rather than mostly effective.
See above: the research question is clearly addressed with a balanced overall verdict, satisfying the top band for answering the RQ.

The student uses the stakeholder concept, CSR and CBM as basic tools, but does not employ more analytical frameworks (e.g. Triple Bottom Line, Blake & Mouton, decision trees).  Selection is therefore limited, fitting the band where âsome tools and theoriesâ are chosen.

The conclusion restates key findings and explicitly answers the question by judging SMC as âsubstantially ethicalâ¦yet imperfect.â  Statements align with the evidence given (training programmes, emissions data, material-mix decline), fulfilling the highest descriptor for this strand.

Examples, statistics and policy references are drawn directly from Stella McCartneyâs 2023 Impact Report, collaborations with EFI, Protein Evolution and The RealReal, demonstrating sustained linkage to the organisation.  Still, some comments (e.g. generic definition of suppliers or employees) drift into textbook description, so the integration with the firm is strong but not seamless.

The student consistently returns to the key concept of ethics when discussing suppliers, supply-chain workers, CSR and circular business models.  Ethical definitions are stated and then revisited in each section, showing solid conceptual grasp.  However, the discussion often shifts into description of actions rather than analysing how these actions exemplify, conflict with, or redefine ethical norms, so integration is only partial, not âthroughoutâ.

annotations.comment: The research question is clear and focused on ethical practices. To sharpen scope, explicitly include âfrom 2022 onwardsâ in the question to match your stated timeframe and ensure alignment throughout the IA.
Explicitly stating âKey Concept: Ethicsâ demonstrates clear conceptual focus from the outset, guiding your analytical lens throughout the report.
Well-structured table of contents outlines a logical flow of sections, facilitating reader navigation and demonstrating strong organization.
Include page numbers in your table of contents to guide the reader more effectively and adhere to IB structure requirements.
The opening contextual background effectively situates Stella McCartney within the fashion industry and highlights her ethical positioning, setting a strong stage for the research.
The definition of ethics is surface-level and not tied to your analysis. Elaborate on ethical frameworks (e.g., utilitarianism, deontology) to underpin evaluation of SMCâs actions.
Consider refining the introduction to explicitly state the scope and research objectives (e.g., focus on policies from 2022 onwards) for greater clarity and coherence.
Consider using a stakeholder power-interest matrix to prioritise stakeholder groups and enhance analytical depth in your stakeholder analysis beyond descriptive listing.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Good use of SD2 data to illustrate supplier training initiatives, demonstrating evidence-based evaluation of ethical practices.
The discussion of workersâ conditions is descriptive; include quantitative or qualitative survey data to support claims about working conditions and their ethical implications.
The image is relevant but lacks analytical commentary; interpret how the Raffia bag design embodies ethical considerations in SMCâs CSR strategy.
Clear definition of CSR and structured categorisation of CSR impacts provides a solid foundation for analysis.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: Effective case study application of Raffia bags illustrates local impact and aligns CSR theory with a real-world example.
Good linkage of CBM concept to SMCâs circular initiatives shows understanding of theory-to-practice application.
annotations.groupName: Selection and Application of Tools and Theories

annotations.comment: The description of the recycled polyester collaboration is informative but could be deepened by evaluating sustainability trade-offs and life-cycle implications.
Insightful discussion of The RealReal collaboration shows understanding of product life-cycle extension within a circular business model.
The quantitative table is useful; enhance analysis by linking percentage changes to ethical performance benchmarks or stakeholder outcomes.
Observation of the material percentage decline is important, yet the analysis stops; discuss potential causes and strategic implications for SMCâs ethical commitments.
Critical reflection on responsible material metrics demonstrates integration of conflicting evidence into your overall evaluation.
The conclusion clearly marks the transition to synthesising findings, fulfilling structural expectations for summarising results.
The conclusion succinctly restates key findings and aligns them with the research question, demonstrating consistency with the evidence.
To strengthen concluding remarks, consider offering specific recommendations for SMCâs future ethical improvements rather than broad statements.
You have clearly formatted and numbered five supporting documents, satisfying the requirement and demonstrating careful document selection.
Most supporting documents are SMC or industry sources; have you considered including academic or NGO reports to diversify perspectives and deepen analysis?
References show inconsistent formatting and broken URL lines; ensure consistent citation style and correct line breaks to improve presentation.
The reference list is comprehensive and includes reputable sources for both theoretical concepts and company data, supporting credibility.
In-text citations should include consistent formatting (e.g., page numbers or author-year) to improve traceability between text and references.
